1:
παρακαλῶ οὖν ὑμᾶς, ἀδελφοί, διὰ τῶν οἰκτιρμῶν τοῦ θεοῦ — The oun (therefore) is the structural hinge linking chs. 1–11 to chs. 12–16: the entire doctrinal argument grounds the ethical appeal. Parakaleō (G3870, urge, exhort) is softer than command; the motivation clause dia tōn oiktirmōn (through the mercies of God) invokes chs. 9–11's mercy-theme. Oiktirmos (G3628, compassion) is plural here — the many acts of divine mercy form the basis of the appeal.

παραστῆσαι τὰ σώματα ὑμῶν θυσίαν ζῶσαν — The sacrificial vocabulary is deliberate: paristēmi (G3936, to present/offer, the term for presenting animals at the altar), thysia (G2378, sacrifice). But the sacrifice is zōsan (living) — not slaughtered. Hagian euareston tō theō (holy and pleasing to God) picks up the Levitical acceptability language transferred to bodily offering. τὴν λογικὴν λατρείαν ὑμῶν — Logikēn (G3050) derives from logos: what corresponds to reason, the rational/verbal offering. It is distinguished from external cultic rites (latreia, G2999, temple service) that are now replaced by embodied, rational devotion.
2:
μὴ συσχηματίζεσθε τῷ αἰῶνι τούτῳ — Syschēmatizō (G4964) denotes conforming to an external pattern, shaping oneself to a mold. Aión (G165) here is the present age in its temporal and moral character — the existing order with its values, ambitions, and assumptions. ἀλλὰ μεταμορφοῦσθε τῇ ἀνακαινώσει τοῦ νοός — Metamorphoō (G3339, transform) differs: it describes an inward structural change, not merely surface alteration. Anakainōsis (G342, renewal/renovation) is a hapax cognate: the noun refers to the process of making new from within. The agent is nous (G3563, mind/moral reason): the renewed faculty of discernment makes possible the proving of God's will.

εἰς τὸ δοκιμάζειν ὑμᾶς τί τὸ θέλημα τοῦ θεοῦ — Dokimazein (G1381, to test/prove/discern) is the word for assaying metal: the renewed mind is capable of accurate moral discernment. The three-fold description of God's will — to agathon kai euareston kai teleion (good, pleasing, and perfect) — is chiastic with 12:1: what is offered must be euareston (pleasing); what is discerned is the same euareston will.
3: μὴ ὑπερφρονεῖν παρ᾽ ὃ δεῖ φρονεῖν — Hyperphronein (to think above what one ought) and sōphronein (to think soberly) both derive from phronein (to think, to set one's mind). Paul creates a word-play: the excess of thinking highly must be restrained to the measure. The measure is κατὰ τὸ μέτρον τῆς πίστεως ὃ ἐμέρισεν ὁ θεός — the measure of faith God distributed. The metron (measure) is not self-evaluated; God distributes (emerisen, aorist) it. This grounds humility not in self-deprecation but in accurate calibration against the divine allotment.
4: ἐν γὰρ τῷ σώματι τῷ ἑνὶ πολλὰ μέλη ἔχομεν — The body (sōma, G4983) metaphor will be developed extensively at 1 Cor 12. Here it is compressed: one body, many members (melē, G3196), different functions (praxis, G4234, action/activity). The physical body is the analogy, not allegory: the argument depends on the observable fact of functional differentiation within organic unity.
5: οἱ πολλοὶ ἓν σῶμά ἐσμεν ἐν Χριστῷ — Hoi polloi (the many) becomes hen sōma (one body): plurality does not dissolve but is constituted as unity en Christō. The phrase to de kath᾽ heis allelōn melē (each one members of one another) expresses mutual belonging: the members are not merely parts of a whole but relational participants — belonging to each other.
6: ἔχοντες δὲ χαρίσματα κατὰ τὴν χάριν τὴν δοθεῖσαν ἡμῖν διάφορα — Charismata (G5486, gifts of grace) differ from one another: diaphora (different, various). The standard for exercise is kata tēn analogian tēs pisteōs (according to the proportion of faith): analogia (G356, proportion, ratio) is a mathematical term. Prophecy is to be exercised in proportion to one's measure of faith — the output calibrated to the input.
7: εἴτε διακονίαν, ἐν τῇ διακονίᾳ — The gift list is structured by eite (either… or) + noun + activity. Diakonia (G1248, service/ministry) — in service; didaskalia (G1319, teaching) — in teaching. The parallel structure in each clause: name the gift, then describe its exercise. The list is not exhaustive (cf. 1 Cor 12) but illustrative of the principle that each gift has a corresponding mode of exercise.
8: ὁ μεταδιδοὺς ἐν ἁπλότητι — Metadidous (the one sharing/giving, G3330): giving is a charisma here. Haplotēs (G572, simplicity/sincerity) — without ulterior motive or self-promotion. Proïstamenos (G4291, leader/one who stands before): leadership exercised en spoudē (with diligence). Eleon (G1656, mercy, compassionate act) — mercy shown en hilarotēti (G2432, hilarity/cheerfulness, the root of English "hilarious"): the etymology preserves the connection — mercy given without resentment.
9:
ἡ ἀγάπη ἀνυπόκριτος — The paraenesis begins with a nominative absolute, a statement about love functioning as a heading. Agapē (G26, dl=3) is the covenant-loyalty, self-giving form of love. Anypokritos (G505, without hypocrisy, unfeigned) is a single compound adjective: a- (not) + hypokrinō (to play a role, from theater). Love that is not theatrical performance.

ἀποστυγοῦντες τὸ πονηρόν, κολλώμενοι τῷ ἀγαθῷ — The participles apostygountes (abhorring, G655 — apo + stygein, to hate with a shudder) and kollōmenoi (clinging, G2853, a word for adhesion/gluing) are in sharp contrast. The strength of the language: not merely avoiding evil but detesting it; not merely pursuing good but adhering to it.
10: τῇ φιλαδελφίᾳ εἰς ἀλλήλους φιλόστοργοι — Philadelphia (G5360, brotherly love) and philostorgos (G5387, tenderly devoted — as family affection): Paul applies family-affection vocabulary to the community. Tē timē allēlous proēgoumenoi — in honor outstripping one another: the verb proēgeomai (G4285) is competitive — each racing to honor the other first. The competitive spirit is directed toward deferring rather than receiving honor.
11: τῇ σπουδῇ μὴ ὀκνηροί, τῷ πνεύματι ζέοντες — Oknēros (G3636, sluggish, lazy): not negligent in eagerness. Zeontes tō pneumati (burning/boiling in spirit, G2204): the thermal metaphor for spiritual fervor — the same word for boiling liquid. Whether pneuma here is the Holy Spirit or the human spirit is debated; the dative of sphere (in spirit) suggests it is the sphere of operation. Tō kyriō douleuontes (serving the Lord): the present participle marks ongoing service as the context of zeal.
12: τῇ ἐλπίδι χαίροντες, τῇ θλίψει ὑπομένοντες — Three datives of sphere, three participles: rejoicing in hope, enduring in affliction, persevering in prayer. Hypomonē (the noun root behind hypomenontes, G5278) is not passive resignation but active endurance under load. Prosedreountes tē proseuchē (G4342, continuing steadfastly in prayer): the compound pros + hedraios (firmly seated before) — holding one's position at prayer.
13: ταῖς χρείαις τῶν ἁγίων κοινωνοῦντες — Koinōneō (G2841, to share in/contribute to): the verb behind koinōnia. The object tais khreiais (the needs) of the saints — practical need-sharing is part of the community's identity, not an optional charity. Tēn philoxenian diōkontes (G5381, pursue hospitality): diōkō is the pursuit-word, normally used for chasing an enemy or a prize. Hospitality is to be pursued, not merely practiced when convenient.
14: εὐλογεῖτε τοὺς διώκοντας ὑμᾶς — The echo of Q-tradition (Matt 5:44 // Luke 6:28) is close enough that Paul may be quoting dominical teaching without identifying it as such. The present tense eulogein (to bless, speak well of) and the command not to curse (mē katarasthe) cover the same action from both sides. The ones who pursue/persecute (diōkontas) are the same word as "pursue" in v.13: the linguistic echo sharpens the irony.
15: χαίρειν μετὰ χαιρόντων, κλαίειν μετὰ κλαιόντων — Two infinitives used as imperatives (asyndeton — no connecting particle). Klaiein (to weep, G2799) with those who weep: Paul places this second, perhaps because it is less natural. Empathic sorrow with the grieving requires more self-transcendence than shared joy. The emotional mirroring expected of the community is comprehensive — celebration and grief.
16: τὸ αὐτὸ εἰς ἀλλήλους φρονοῦντες — To auto phronein (to think the same thing toward one another): the phrase does not mandate intellectual uniformity but the same orientation of mutual regard. μὴ τὰ ὑψηλὰ φρονοῦντες ἀλλὰ τοῖς ταπεινοῖς συναπαγόμενοι — Hypsēla phronein (to think high/lofty thoughts) versus tois tapeinois synapagomenoi (carried away with the lowly). Tapeinoi (G5011) may mean humble people or low social position — both senses apply. Synapagō (G4879, to be carried away with/toward) suggests being drawn into their world, not merely condescending to help them.
17: μηδενὶ κακὸν ἀντὶ κακοῦ ἀποδιδόντες — Anti (in exchange for): the tit-for-tat structure of retribution is the thing prohibited. Apodidōmi (G591, to give back) used of rendering what is owed: treating evil as something that creates an obligation to return evil. προνοούμενοι καλὰ ἐνώπιον πάντων ἀνθρώπων — providing for good (pronoumenoi, G4306, to think beforehand/plan for) in the sight of all people: visible integrity as a community value, not merely private virtue.
18: εἰ δυνατόν, τὸ ἐξ ὑμῶν, μετὰ πάντων ἀνθρώπων εἰρηνεύοντες — Two qualifications: ei dynaton (if possible — not always possible) and to ex hymōn (as far as it is from you — contingent on your own side). Paul is realistic: peace with all people is the aim, but both conditions recognize that the will of others may make it unachievable. The imperative mood of eirēneuontes applies to what lies within the community's own power.
19: μὴ ἑαυτοὺς ἐκδικοῦντες, ἀγαπητοί — Ekdikeō (G1556, to vindicate, avenge) — the reflexive construction: do not avenge yourselves. Agapētoi (beloved) is an unusual address in Paul's ethical sections; it signals the warmth behind the prohibition. δότε τόπον τῇ ὀργῇ — give place to the wrath: orgē with the article may indicate God's wrath specifically. The citation follows from Deut 32:35: "mine is the vengeance, I will repay." Kyrios (Lord) — in the LXX this is YHWH; Paul applies the same principle to Christian conduct.
20: ἐὰν πεινᾷ ὁ ἐχθρός σου, ψώμιζε αὐτόν — Prov 25:21–22 (LXX). The feeding and giving drink are the counter-action to enmity. ἄνθρακας πυρὸς σωρεύσεις ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν αὐτοῦ — Anthrakas pyros (coals of fire): the image is debated — shame (heat on the head as a sign of burning remorse), an Egyptian ritual of repentance, or simply the effect that kindness has in exposing and intensifying the enemy's guilt. The Proverbs context suggests the purpose is the enemy's transformation, not punishment: the LXX adds "and the Lord will reward you," framing the act as generosity.
21: μὴ νικῶ ὑπὸ τοῦ κακοῦ, ἀλλὰ νίκα ἐν τῷ ἀγαθῷ τὸ κακόν — The closing aphorism is chiastic: nikō/nikō (overcome) and kakō/kalon (evil/good). The verb nikaō (G3528, to conquer, used of military victory) frames the ethical life as agonistic: evil is a conquering force; the response is counter-conquest through good. The preposition en (in/by means of) marks the instrument: the weapon against evil is good itself.
